metrics.reason: Directly indicates whether the sshd process is running; zero means the SSH service is down.
Shows if the SSH port (22) is listening; if not, clients cannot connect even if sshd is alive.
Number of active SSH socket connections; helps verify if the socket is functional.
Rate of accepted SSH connections; a drop to zero may indicate a socket or firewall issue.
Firewall status; if inactive or mis‑configured it could block port 22.
DNS resolver service; SSH may fail if name resolution is broken.
High I/O wait can stall sshd responsiveness and cause it to terminate.
Overall CPU availability; low idle may indicate resource starvation affecting sshd.
Memory pressure can cause the sshd process to be killed by the OOM killer.
Swap exhaustion can also lead to OOM conditions impacting sshd.
Recent reboot could explain a missing sshd process if the service failed to start.
Correlates uptime with service start times for timeline analysis.
Inbound interface errors may prevent SSH packets from being received.
Outbound interface errors may prevent SSH responses from being sent.
Dropped inbound packets can indicate network congestion or mis‑configuration affecting SSH.
Dropped outbound packets can also impact SSH session stability.
Maximum allowed processes; hitting this limit can prevent sshd from spawning.
Maximum file descriptors; exhaustion can stop sshd from accepting new connections.
